name: Pitcairn Islands

colors.symbolism: the field of the British Blue Ensign
the saltire and cross of the Union Flag in the canton; details of the territorial arms
the cross of Saint George and saltire of Saint Patrick in the Union Flag canton
the green of the territorial shield, representing the islands' vegetation
the gold of the anchor and Bible, recalling HMS Bounty and the mutineers' descendants
the blue chief of the territorial shield, representing the surrounding Pacific Ocean
the wheelbarrow and miro tree of the helm crest

description: A British Blue Ensign — a navy field with the Union Flag in the upper-hoist canton — defaced in the fly with the Pitcairn coat of arms (a green shield with blue chief bearing a yellow anchor and Bible, surmounted by a helm with a miro plant in a wheelbarrow).
history: Granted by Queen Elizabeth II on 2 April 1984. Pitcairn was settled in 1790 by mutineers from HMS Bounty (whence the anchor and Bible in the arms) and their Tahitian companions; it became a British settlement in 1838 and is now the only remaining British Overseas Territory in the Pacific. The Pitcairn Islands include Pitcairn (the only inhabited island), Henderson, Ducie and Oeno.

features.symbolism: navy field of the British Blue Ensign — Pitcairn's flag is a defaced Blue Ensign indicating its status as a British Overseas Territory
Pitcairn's status as a British Overseas Territory

features.role: Coat of arms of the Pitcairn Islands
features.symbolism: a green shield with a blue chief bearing a yellow anchor (representing HMS Bounty, on which the islands' settlers mutinied in 1789) and a yellow open Bible (the Bounty Bible, central to the islands' religious community); a helm with a wheelbarrow and miro plant above
